questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An AC source generating an emf $\mathcal{E} = \mathcal{E}_0 \left[\cos\!\left((100\pi \text{ s}^{-1})t\right) + \cos\!\left((500\pi \text{ s}^{-1})t\right)\right]$ is connected in series with a resistor and a capacitor. The steady-state current flowing in the circuit is observed to be $i = i_1 \cos\!\left[(100\pi \text{ s}^{-1})t + \phi_1\right] + i_2 \cos\!\left[(500\pi \text{ s}^{-1})t + \phi_2\right]$.
options: The information is insufficient to determine the relationship between $i_1$ and $i_2$
questions.solution:
The given emf has two frequency components: $\omega_1 = 100\pi \text{ s}^{-1}$ and $\omega_2 = 500\pi \text{ s}^{-1}$.

The impedance of an RC series circuit at an angular frequency $\omega$ is given by $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} = \sqrt{R^2 + \dfrac{1}{\omega^2 C^2}}$.

The amplitude of the steady-state current at frequency $\omega$ is $i_0 = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_0}{Z} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_0}{\sqrt{R^2 + \dfrac{1}{\omega^2 C^2}}}$.

Since $\omega_1 < \omega_2$, the capacitive reactance at $\omega_1$ is greater than at $\omega_2$, which means $\dfrac{1}{\omega_1^2 C^2} > \dfrac{1}{\omega_2^2 C^2}$.

This implies that the impedance at $\omega_1$ is greater than the impedance at $\omega_2$, i.e., $Z_1 > Z_2$.

Therefore, the current amplitude at $\omega_1$ is less than the current amplitude at $\omega_2$, giving $i_1 < i_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An AC source carries a rating of $220 \text{ V}$, $50 \text{ Hz}$. When the average voltage is evaluated over a time interval of $0.01 \text{ s}$, this average
questions.solution:
Frequency of the AC source, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$

Time period, $T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s}$

The given time interval for evaluating the average voltage is $\Delta t = 0.01 \text{ s}$, which is exactly $\dfrac{T}{2}$.

The instantaneous voltage of the AC source can be represented as $V = V_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$.

The average voltage over an interval $\Delta t$ from $t_1$ to $t_1 + \Delta t$ is given by:

$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t} \int_{t_1}^{t_1 + \Delta t} V_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi) dt$

Since $\Delta t = \dfrac{T}{2}$, the integral evaluates the area under the voltage curve over half a cycle.

If the time interval starts at $t_1$ such that $\omega t_1 + \phi = 0$, the average voltage is $\dfrac{2V_0}{\pi}$.

However, if the time interval is chosen such that it starts at a peak, i.e., $\omega t_1 + \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, the interval spans from phase $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ to $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$. The positive and negative areas under the curve in this interval are equal and opposite, making the integral zero.

Thus, the average voltage over a time interval of $0.01 \text{ s}$ depends on the starting time and may be zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When connected to an AC source, the magnetic field energy within an inductor transitions from its maximum to its minimum value in $5.0 \text{ ms}$. The frequency of the AC source is
questions.solution:
The magnetic energy stored in an inductor is given by $U = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$

For an AC circuit, the current varies as $I = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$

Substituting the value of current, we get $U = \dfrac{1}{2} L I_0^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$

The magnetic energy is maximum when $\sin^2(\omega t) = 1$ and minimum when $\sin^2(\omega t) = 0$.

The phase difference between the maximum and minimum values is $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

The time taken for this transition is $\Delta t = \dfrac{T}{4}$, where $T$ is the time period of the AC source.

Given $\Delta t = 5.0 \text{ ms} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$

$\dfrac{T}{4} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$

$T = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$

The frequency of the AC source is $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The peak voltage of a $220 \text{ V}$ AC source is
questions.solution:
The given voltage of an AC source is its RMS (root mean square) value.

$V_{rms} = 220 \text{ V}$

The peak voltage $V_{0}$ is related to the RMS voltage by the formula:

$V_{0} = \sqrt{2} V_{rms}$

Substituting the given value:

$V_{0} = 1.414 \times 220 \text{ V}$

$V_{0} \approx 311 \text{ V}$

Thus, the peak voltage is about $310 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor behaves as an infinite resistance for
questions.solution:
The capacitive reactance of a capacitor is given by $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$, where $f$ is the frequency of the current.

For direct current (DC), the frequency $f = 0$.

Substituting $f = 0$ in the formula, we get $X_C = \infty$.

Thus, a capacitor offers infinite resistance to DC and blocks it completely.

For alternating current (AC), $f > 0$, so $X_C$ has a finite value.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the given plots can represent the reactance of a series $LC$ combination?
questions.solution:
The inductive reactance is given by $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$. The plot of $X_L$ versus frequency $f$ is a straight line passing through the origin, which corresponds to curve a.

The capacitive reactance is given by $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$. The plot of $X_C$ versus frequency $f$ is a rectangular hyperbola, which corresponds to curves b and c.

For a series $LC$ combination, the net reactance is the difference between the capacitive and inductive reactances. If we consider the net reactance as $X = X_C - X_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} - 2\pi f L$:
- At low frequencies ($f \to 0$), the capacitive reactance dominates, so $X \to \infty$.
- At high frequencies ($f \to \infty$), the inductive reactance dominates, so $X \to -\infty$.
- At the resonant frequency $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, $X_C = X_L$, so $X = 0$.

Curve d starts at a large positive value, crosses the frequency axis (where reactance becomes zero), and then goes into the negative region. This perfectly matches the behavior of $X_C - X_L$.

Therefore, curve d represents the reactance of a series $LC$ combination.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A series AC circuit possesses a resistance of $4 	ext{ }\Omega$ and a reactance of $3 	ext{ }\Omega$. The impedance of the circuit is
questions.solution:
Given resistance $R = 4 \Omega$ and reactance $X = 3 \Omega$.

The impedance $Z$ of a series AC circuit is given by:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$

Substituting the given values:

$Z = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2}$

$Z = \sqrt{16 + 9}$

$Z = \sqrt{25}$

$Z = 5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An alternating current is expressed as $i = i_1 \cos \omega t + i_2 \sin \omega t$. The rms value of this current is
questions.solution:
The given alternating current is $i = i_1 \cos \omega t + i_2 \sin \omega t$.

The rms value of the current is given by $i_{rms} = \sqrt{\langle i^2 \rangle}$, where $\langle i^2 \rangle$ is the mean square value of the current over a complete cycle.

Squaring the current expression:

$i^2 = (i_1 \cos \omega t + i_2 \sin \omega t)^2$

$i^2 = i_1^2 \cos^2 \omega t + i_2^2 \sin^2 \omega t + 2 i_1 i_2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t$

Taking the average over one complete cycle:

$\langle i^2 \rangle = i_1^2 \langle \cos^2 \omega t \rangle + i_2^2 \langle \sin^2 \omega t \rangle + i_1 i_2 \langle \sin 2\omega t \rangle$

Since the average values over a full cycle are $\langle \cos^2 \omega t \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}$, $\langle \sin^2 \omega t \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}$, and $\langle \sin 2\omega t \rangle = 0$, we get:

$\langle i^2 \rangle = i_1^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) + i_2^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) + 0 = \dfrac{i_1^2 + i_2^2}{2}$

Therefore, the rms value is:

$i_{rms} = \sqrt{\langle i^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\dfrac{i_1^2 + i_2^2}{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal wire is heated by an alternating current with a peak value of $14 \text{ A}$. To achieve the same heating effect, a constant current $i$ can be applied where $i$ is
questions.solution:
The heating effect of an alternating current is equivalent to the heating effect of a direct (constant) current equal to its root mean square (RMS) value.

The RMS value of an alternating current is given by $I_{rms} = \dfrac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}$

Given the peak current $I_0 = 14 \text{ A}$

$I_{rms} = \dfrac{14}{\sqrt{2}} = 7\sqrt{2} \text{ A}$

Substituting $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$

$I_{rms} = 7 \times 1.414 = 9.898 \text{ A}$

This is approximately $10 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A resistor carries a constant current of $2.8 \text{ A}$. The rms value of this current is
questions.solution:
The root mean square (rms) value of a current $I(t)$ over a time interval $T$ is given by $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I^{2} dt}$.

For a constant current, $I(t) = I_{0}$ for all $t$.

Substituting this into the formula, we get $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I_{0}^{2} dt} = \sqrt{I_{0}^{2}} = I_{0}$.

Given that the constant current is $2.8 \text{ A}$, its rms value is also $2.8 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
options: in both DC and AC circuits
in neither DC nor AC circuits
questions.solution:
A transformer operates on the principle of mutual induction, which requires a continuously changing magnetic flux to induce an electromotive force (EMF) in the secondary coil.

In an AC circuit, the alternating current produces a varying magnetic field, allowing the transformer to function. In a DC circuit, the steady current produces a constant magnetic field, resulting in zero induced EMF in the secondary coil.

Therefore, transformers are employed solely in AC circuits.
